ccel.title: NPNF2-09. Hilary of Poitiers, John of Damascus

source.title: NPNF2-09. Hilary of Poitiers, John of Damascus

title: Concerning His Fear.

source.title: NPNF2-09. Hilary of Poitiers, John of Damascus

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: The word fear has a double meaning.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For fear is natural when the soul is unwilling to be separated from the body, on account of the natural sympathy and close relationship planted in it in the beginning by the Creator, which makes it fear and struggle against death and pray for an escape from it.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: It may be defined thus: natural fear is the force whereby we cling to being with shrinking.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For if all things were brought by the Creator out of nothing into being, they all have by nature a longing after being and not after non-being.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Moreover the inclination towards those things that support existence is a natural property of them.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Hence God the Word when He became man had this longing, manifesting, on the one hand, in those things that support existence, the inclination of His nature in desiring food and drink and sleep, and having in a natural manner made proof of these things, while on the other hand displaying in those things that bring corruption His natural disinclination in voluntarily shrinking in the hour of His passion before the face of death.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For although what happened did so according to the laws of nature, yet it was not, as in our case, a matter of necessity.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For He willingly and spontaneously accepted that which was natural.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: So that fear itself and terror and agony belong to the natural and innocent passions and are not under the dominion of sin.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Again, there is a fear which arises from treachery of reasoning and want of faith, and ignorance of the hour of death, as when we are at night affected by fear at some chance noise.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: This is unnatural fear, and may be thus defined: unnatural fear is an unexpected shrinking.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: This our Lord did not assume.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Hence He never felt fear except in the hour of His passion, although He often experienced a feeling of shrinking in accordance with the dispensation.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For He was not ignorant of the appointed time.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: But the holy Athanasius in his discourse against Apollinarius says that He did actually feel fear.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: “Wherefore the Lord said: Now is My soul troubled.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: The ‘now’ indeed means just ‘when He willed,’ but yet points to what actually was.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For He did not speak of what was not, as though it were present, as if the things that were said only apparently happened.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For all things happened naturally and actually.”

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: And again, after some other matters, he says, “In nowise does His divinity admit passion apart from a suffering body, nor yet does it manifest trouble and pain apart from a pained and troubled soul, nor does it suffer anguish and offer up prayer apart from a mind that suffered anguish and offered up prayer.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For, although these occurrences were not due to any overthrow of nature, yet they took place to shew forth His real being.”

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: The words “these occurrences were not due to any overthrow of His nature,” prove that it was not involuntarily that He endured these things.
